sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Autonomic dysreflexia is a hypertensive emergency seen mainly in patients with spinal cord injury, usually at or above T6. It is triggered by noxious stimuli below the lesion and can rapidly lead to intracranial haemorrhage, seizure, dysrhythmia, or cardiac complications if not recognised and relieved.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Known spinal cord injury, often above T6
- Sudden severe headache with systolic blood pressure 20 mmHg or more above baseline, or marked hypertension such as 160 mmHg or higher if baseline is unknown
- Flushing or sweating above the lesion with pallor or cool skin below it
- Bradycardia or less commonly tachycardia
- Anxiety, blurred vision, chest discomfort, dyspnoea, or reduced conscious state in severe cases

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Common triggers include bladder distension, blocked catheter, bowel distension, pressure injury, fractures, burns, tight clothing, or other painful stimuli
- Symptoms may improve after the trigger is relieved, but the patient still needs medical assessment because recurrence is possible
- Consider stroke, ACS, or other hypertensive emergencies if the diagnosis is uncertain
- Obtain the patient's usual resting blood pressure if known because a modest absolute value may still represent severe hypertension for that individual

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Sit the patient upright if tolerated to promote an orthostatic reduction in blood pressure
- Loosen restrictive clothing or equipment and limit exertion as much as possible
- Search for and remove obvious noxious stimuli where safe and within scope, such as a twisted drainage line or painful pressure point
- Give oxygen only if clinically indicated
- Reassess blood pressure, pain, and symptoms frequently and transport even if symptoms settle

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Continue structured search for reversible triggers, including bladder and bowel causes, within scope and dignity constraints
- Provide analgesia within scope where pain is a likely trigger or consequence
- Consider protocol-led vasodilator therapy only within local scope or senior direction if severe hypertension persists after simple trigger relief
- Escalate early if neurological symptoms, chest pain, refractory hypertension, or recurrent symptoms are present

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Repeated or prolonged autonomic dysreflexia can cause intracranial haemorrhage, seizure, myocardial injury, or dysrhythmia
- Ask the patient or carer about their usual trigger pattern and any existing management plan
- Document blood pressure trend, suspected trigger, interventions performed, and whether symptoms resolved before arrival
